title: The skipped newborn screen and the fair-haired baby
blurb: An untreated home-birth infant presenting at 8 months with developmental delay, a musty odor, and a pale phenotype her parents never expected.

patient.complaint: Not meeting milestones; mom thinks she smells 'mousy'.

opening: 8-month-old, born at home, never had the newborn screen done. Pediatrician sees her today for a delayed visit. She isn't rolling, has poor head control, and tracks faces only intermittently. Parents are both brown-haired with hazel eyes; she has very fair skin, blond hair, and bright-blue eyes. Mom mentions a faint musty odor in her sweat that has been there since birth. What do you want to ask, examine, or order next?
history: Home birth, mom declined newborn screening. Pregnancy uneventful. Two older siblings are healthy and dark-haired. No maternal medications or maternal hyperphenylalaninemia. Diet: breast milk and some pureed cereals.
exam: Vitals normal for age. Anthropometrics: head circumference at 5th centile (was 30th at birth — microcephaly trend). Fair skin, blond hair, blue eyes, eczema on cheeks. Hypertonic with brisk reflexes. Persistent tongue thrust. No dysmorphism.

labs.name: Brain MRI
labs.value: Mild diffuse white matter signal abnormality

prompts.diagnosis: What is the diagnosis (and please say whether classical or BH₄-responsive)?
prompts.mechanism: Briefly: why are the hair and skin pale, and why the developmental regression?

pinpoint.prompt: Click the enzyme step that fails in classical PKU.
pinpoint.correctMsg: Yes — PAH (phenylalanine hydroxylase), BH₄-dependent. Phe can't be hydroxylated to Tyr, so Phe and its abnormal metabolites (phenylpyruvate, phenylacetate, phenyllactate) accumulate. Tyr becomes essential — and melanin synthesis suffers because tyrosinase needs Tyr.
pinpoint.wrongMsg: Not quite. Look at the very first step that converts Phe into the next amino acid in the catecholamine cascade.
pinpoint.revealMsg: Target: PAH (BH₄) — Phenylalanine → Tyrosine. Note the BH₄ cofactor: defects in BH₄ synthesis or recycling (DHPR, GTPCH1, PTPS, SR) mimic PKU but with additional CNS features.

mcqs.stem: Why does PKU produce the fair / blond / blue-eyed phenotype?
choices: Tyrosinase requires tyrosine to make melanin; low tyrosine blocks melanin synthesis
BH₄ is required for melanin synthesis
mcqs.explanation: Tyrosinase converts tyrosine → DOPA → DOPAquinone → melanin. With low circulating tyrosine and (locally) impaired tyrosinase, melanin synthesis is reduced. Hair, skin, and iris pigmentation lighten.

mcqs.stem: What is the cornerstone of treatment?
choices: Strict low-phenylalanine diet (medical formula, restricted natural protein, no aspartame); supplement tyrosine; BH₄ (sapropterin) if responsive
mcqs.explanation: Dietary phenylalanine restriction is lifelong, with specialized formula providing protein and other amino acids. Supplement tyrosine. Some PKU variants are BH₄-responsive (sapropterin = BH₄ analog). Avoid aspartame (it's hydrolyzed to phenylalanine).

mcqs.stem: A different child with HIGH phenylalanine, LOW tyrosine, abnormal urine pterins, and dystonia has NORMAL PAH gene sequencing. What's the likely defect?
choices: A defect in BH₄ synthesis or recycling (e.g., DHPR, GTPCH1, PTPS, SR deficiency)
mcqs.explanation: BH₄ is also the cofactor for tyrosine hydroxylase, tryptophan hydroxylase, and NO synthase. A BH₄ disorder gives PKU-like Phe / Tyr labs PLUS dopamine and serotonin deficiency → dystonia, drooling, autonomic features. Treatment adds BH₄ and neurotransmitter precursors (L-DOPA + carbidopa, 5-HTP).
